column1: #deck:k8s-platform-labs::12-rbac::01-rbac-fundamentals
#separator:Comma
#columns:Front
column2: Back

column1: You run kubectl auth can-i get pods -n ci --as=ci-pipeline and get 'no', even though you're sure you created a RoleBinding. What's the single most likely cause?
column2: The RoleBinding's roleRef.name doesn't match an existing Role's name (typo). kubectl apply succeeds on a RoleBinding with a bad roleRef with no error — it applies cleanly but grants nothing. Verify with kubectl get role -n ci and compare exactly.

column1: A Role's rule has verbs: ["get"] only. kubectl get pods (no name) still fails for that subject even though single-Pod fetches work. Why?
column2: list and watch are not implied by get. 'kubectl get pods' with no name performs a list call under the hood, which was never granted. All three verbs are usually needed together.

column1: A Role rule uses apiGroups: ["core"] for a Pods rule, and the grant silently matches nothing. What's wrong?
column2: The core API group's correct value is the empty string "", not the literal word "core". "core" is an informal label, not the actual apiGroup identifier — Kubernetes accepts any string in apiGroups without validating it exists.

column1: You need a subject to read Pod logs, and you've granted resources: ["pods"] with verbs get/list/watch. Log access still fails. What's missing?
column2: pods/log is a separate resource (a subresource) from pods. Granting access to pods does not include access to pods/log — it must be listed explicitly in the resources array.

column1: What's the practical difference between a Role and a ClusterRole with an identical rules block?
column2: A Role is scoped to the namespace it's created in and can never grant access outside it, even with an overly broad rule. A ClusterRole with the same rules, if bound via ClusterRoleBinding, grants that access across every namespace including kube-system.

column1: Why does RBAC have no explicit deny rule type?
column2: RBAC is designed as strictly additive — effective permissions are the union of every applicable Role/ClusterRole grant. There's no mechanism to subtract from that union; you can only reduce access by removing or narrowing the grant that created it.

column1: You edit a RoleBinding's roleRef to point at a different Role. What happens?
column2: Nothing changes — roleRef is immutable after creation. The API server rejects the edit. You must delete and recreate the RoleBinding to change which Role it references.

column1: kubectl auth can-i delete pods -n ci --as=alice returns yes. Does this guarantee alice's delete request will actually succeed?
column2: No. can-i only evaluates the Authorization stage. Admission Control (quotas, validating webhooks, Pod Security Admission) runs afterward and can still reject the request for reasons unrelated to RBAC.

column1: What does the --as flag on kubectl auth can-i actually do under the hood?
column2: It triggers Kubernetes' impersonation feature — a real API-server-side mechanism gated by the impersonate verb on your own identity. The API server evaluates the request as if the impersonated identity made it, not just as a client-side filter.

column1: A RoleBinding named ci-pipeline-pod-log-reader is created in namespace default, but the Role pod-log-reader lives in namespace ci. What happens when you apply the RoleBinding?
column2: It fails immediately at apply time with NotFound — a RoleBinding's roleRef lookup is scoped to its own namespace, so a same-named Role in a different namespace is invisible to it, even though it exists elsewhere in the cluster.

column1: Is there a kubectl get users command in Kubernetes?
column2: No. Users are not backed by any API object — they're supplied entirely by the authenticator (client cert CN, OIDC claim, etc.) as plain strings. ServiceAccounts, by contrast, are real API objects you can kubectl get.

column1: Can resourceNames be combined with the create verb in a PolicyRule?
column2: No. resourceNames restricts a rule to specific named objects, but at create time the object doesn't exist yet, so there's no name to restrict against.

column1: What's the exam-fast way to create a RoleBinding without writing YAML?
column2: kubectl create rolebinding NAME --role=ROLE_NAME --user=USER_NAME -n NAMESPACE

column1: What's the exam-fast way to create a Role granting get/list/watch on pods without writing YAML?
column2: kubectl create role NAME --verb=get,list,watch --resource=pods -n NAMESPACE

column1: Where does RBAC sit in the AuthN → AuthZ → Admission Control request flow, and what does it never inspect?
column2: RBAC is one implementation of the AuthZ stage — it decides yes/no for a given identity + verb + resource combination. It never inspects the actual content/payload of the request; that's Admission Control's job.

column1: You create a Role but never create a matching RoleBinding. What access does any subject have as a result of that Role?
column2: None. A Role with no RoleBinding pointing at it grants nobody anything — the Role object existing is necessary but not sufficient.

column1: Why is preferring Role+RoleBinding over ClusterRole+ClusterRoleBinding considered a best practice when both could technically express the same grant?
column2: Because a Role's namespace scope physically bounds the blast radius of a mistake — even a rule as broad as resources: ["*"], verbs: ["*"] inside a Role can never touch another namespace, including kube-system. The same rule via ClusterRoleBinding applies cluster-wide.

column1: A rule specifies resources: ["pod"] (singular). What actually gets matched?
column2: Nothing. Resource names in PolicyRules must be plural and lowercase, exactly as kubectl api-resources lists them — the singular form silently matches zero resources with no error.

column1: By default, before any Role or RoleBinding is ever created, what can a new subject do in Kubernetes?
column2: Nothing. There is no implicit allow anywhere in RBAC — every action requires an explicit grant from some Role/ClusterRole + Binding combination. Absent any grant, the answer to every authorization check is no.

column1: Can a single RoleBinding grant a Role to more than one subject at once?
column2: Yes. subjects is a list, not a single value — one RoleBinding can bind the identical Role to several Users, Groups, and ServiceAccounts simultaneously, each getting the same grant.

column1: What does resources: ["*"] mean in a PolicyRule, and why is it dangerous even when your specific test checks still return the expected answers?
column2: It matches every resource in the rule's apiGroups, not just the ones you intended. Spot-checking with can-i for the resources you meant to grant will pass, since they're a subset of what got granted — the over-grant (e.g. Secrets, ConfigMaps) is invisible unless you run a full can-i --list audit or read the YAML directly.

column1: kubectl auth can-i --list shows rows with an empty Resources column and a path like /healthz under Non-Resource URLs. What are these, and can a namespaced Role grant them?
column2: Non-resource URLs are API server endpoints (health checks, version info, discovery paths) not backed by any Kubernetes object, matched via a separate nonResourceURLs field by literal path. They have no namespace to scope to, so nonResourceURLs rules can only appear in a ClusterRole, never a Role.

column1: Is there a kubectl list pods command?
column2: No. list is an API-level verb, not a CLI-level command. kubectl get is the single CLI entry point covering both get and list — passing an object name issues the get verb, omitting one issues list.

column1: What two things does kubectl exec require permission-wise, beyond just being able to see the pod exists?
column2: get on pods (to resolve the target pod) AND create on the pods/exec subresource — exec is implemented as creating a new exec session, not reading one. Both are required; get alone is not sufficient.

column1: Which apiGroup does kubectl run --image=... require create access to, and why not Deployments?
column2: create on pods (core group). Modern kubectl run always creates a bare Pod object directly, never a Deployment — kubectl create deployment is the separate command for that.

column1: You need a Role granting get/list/watch on both pods (core group) and deployments (apps group). Can kubectl create role express this in one invocation?
column2: No. A single kubectl create role call can only target one apiGroups value at a time (inferred from --resource). Two different apiGroups require either two separate kubectl create role invocations against the same Role name, or hand-editing the resulting YAML to add the second rule block.

column1: kubectl -n ci describe role pod-log-reader shows 'deployments.apps' in its output, but the YAML just wrote resources: ["deployments"] under a separate apiGroups: ["apps"] entry. Why the different formatting?
column2: kubectl describe always qualifies non-core resources with their API group in its display output (resource.group), even though the YAML expresses apiGroups and resources as separate fields. This is a display convention, not a difference in what was actually granted.

column1: A manifest creating a Deployment says apiVersion: apps/v1. What does the matching PolicyRule's apiGroups field contain?
column2: Just ["apps"] — the group portion only, never the version. RBAC grants apply across every version of a resource within that group, so the version part of apiVersion has no equivalent in a PolicyRule.

column1: The API server is started with --authorization-mode=Node,RBAC. A request is denied by the Node authorizer but allowed by RBAC. What's the final outcome?
column2: Allowed. Every configured authorizer is consulted, and the request is admitted the moment any one of them returns allow — it's only denied if every configured authorizer returns no opinion or deny.

column1: What's the key architectural difference between the Webhook authorizer and RBAC?
column2: RBAC evaluates entirely inside kube-apiserver against cached Role/RoleBinding objects, with no network hop. Webhook sends a SubjectAccessReview over HTTPS to an external service you run and waits for its response — a genuine per-request network round-trip.

column1: According to what RBAC does NOT do, if a Role grants unlimited create access on Pods, can that subject create 10,000 Pods with no cap?
column2: Yes — RBAC has no concept of resource limits or quotas. A grant is unlimited in quantity unless ResourceQuota/LimitRange (a separate Admission Control mechanism) is configured to cap it.

column1: Does RBAC control how a Secret is stored at the etcd layer?
column2: No. RBAC only controls who is allowed to read a Secret object — it says nothing about storage. Secrets are base64-encoded, not encrypted, at the etcd layer unless encryption-at-rest is separately configured.

column1: Can an RBAC grant restrict what a Pod is allowed to do at runtime, like which syscalls it makes?
column2: No. RBAC governs API requests to the control plane — it has no visibility into in-container runtime behavior. That's the job of SecurityContext / Pod Security Admission.

column1: Does RBAC itself keep a record of who was granted or denied access, over time?
column2: No, not by itself. RBAC only decides allow/deny in the moment, with no history. Knowing what happened over time requires Kubernetes audit logging, a separate opt-in feature configured on the API server.

column1: In the verbs table, what's the difference between update and patch, and which everyday kubectl commands map to patch rather than update?
column2: update (PUT) replaces an entire existing object; patch (PATCH) partially modifies one. kubectl edit, kubectl scale, kubectl set image, and kubectl apply against an object that already exists all map to patch, not update.

column1: What does the deletecollection verb do, and what kubectl command triggers it?
column2: Removes every object of a type matching a selector in one call, with no specific name — kubectl delete pods --all triggers deletecollection, distinct from delete, which removes exactly one named object.

column1: Does kubectl create role NAME --verb=get --resource=pods --dry-run=client -o yaml actually create the Role in the cluster?
column2: No. --dry-run=client only builds and prints the object locally — it never sends a request to the API server. Nothing exists in the cluster until the output is piped or applied separately.

column1: Why is ABAC rarely used in modern Kubernetes clusters, compared to RBAC?
column2: ABAC policies live in a static JSON file readable only at API server startup — no API object, no kubectl get, no dynamic updates without a restart. RBAC's dynamic, declarative, version-controllable model has replaced it for essentially all new clusters.

column1: What makes the Node authorizer different from RBAC, and why is it typically paired with RBAC rather than used alone?
column2: Node authorizer is a fixed, built-in rule — not Role/RoleBinding-based — letting each kubelet touch only objects tied to its own node. It's paired with RBAC (--authorization-mode=Node,RBAC) so every other kind of request still goes through normal, configurable RBAC evaluation.
